sentences.text: Der var engang en kejser, som elskede smukt tøj mere end alt andet.
sentences.translation: Once there was an emperor who loved beautiful clothes more than anything else.

words.grammar: som (relative pronoun, subject or object) · invariable — same form for people and things

sentences.text: En dag kom der to bedragere til byen.
sentences.translation: One day two swindlers came to the town.

words.grammar: en bedrager · bedrageren (the swindler) · bedragere (plural) · bedragerne (the swindlers)

sentences.text: De sagde, at de kunne væve det smukkeste stof i verden.
sentences.translation: They said that they could weave the most beautiful fabric in the world.

words.grammar: væve (to weave) · væver (present) · vævede (past) · har vævet (perfect)

sentences.text: Stoffet var usynligt for alle dumme mennesker, sagde de.
sentences.translation: The fabric was invisible to all stupid people, they said.

words.grammar: usynlig (common) · usynligt (neuter) · usynlige (pl./def.) · u- = negative prefix, synlig = visible

sentences.text: Kejseren betalte dem meget guld og bad dem væve med det samme.
sentences.translation: The emperor paid them a lot of gold and asked them to weave at once.

words.grammar: bede (to ask/request) · bad (past) · har bedt (perfect) · bede nogen gøre noget = ask someone to do something (no "at")

sentences.text: Ministrene så ingenting, men de turde ikke sige det.
sentences.translation: The ministers saw nothing, but they didn't dare say it.

words.grammar: turde (modal: dare) · tør (present) · turde (past) · har turdet (perfect)

sentences.text: Alle var bange for at blive kaldt dumme, så de roste det smukke stof.
sentences.translation: Everyone was afraid of being called stupid, so they praised the beautiful fabric.

words.grammar: være bange for at + infinitive = to be afraid of (doing) something
rose (to praise) · roste (past) · har rost (perfect) · weak verb

sentences.text: Kejseren gik gennem byen i sit nye, usynlige tøj.
sentences.translation: The emperor walked through the town in his new, invisible clothes.

words.grammar: gennem (through) · governs accusative-like position · gå gennem byen = walk through the town

sentences.text: Folket klappede og råbte, fordi ingen turde sige sandheden.
sentences.translation: The people clapped and shouted, because no one dared tell the truth.

words.grammar: fordi (subordinating) · introduces a clause where the verb stays after the subject, but the clause can't stand alone

sentences.text: Pludselig råbte et lille barn: "Men han har jo ikke noget tøj på!"
sentences.translation: Suddenly a small child shouted: "But he isn't wearing any clothes at all!"

words.grammar: jo (modal particle, untranslatable alone) · adds emphasis to something the speaker sees as self-evident
et barn · barnet (the child) · børn (plural, irregular) · børnene (the children)

sentences.text: Hviskingen bredte sig, og snart sagde alle det samme.
sentences.translation: The whisper spread, and soon everyone said the same thing.

words.grammar: brede sig (to spread, reflexive) · breder sig/bredte sig/har bredt sig · weak verb + reflexive pronoun

sentences.text: Kejseren blev rød i ansigtet, men han fortsatte processionen med rank ryg.
sentences.translation: The emperor turned red in the face, but he continued the procession with a straight back.

words.grammar: fortsætte (to continue) · fortsatte (past) · har fortsat (perfect) · weak verb

metadata.adaptedFrom: Adapted from H.C. Andersen (1837), simplified for upper-A2 learners

grammarPoints.explanation: Som introduces a clause describing a noun, acting as the subject inside that clause. It's invariable — used for both people and things.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: De sagde, at de kunne væve det smukkeste stof i verden.
grammarPoints.explanation: At introduces what someone said as a clause rather than a direct quote. The word order inside stays subject-verb, just like a normal statement.

grammarPoints.rule: Bare infinitive after verbs of request: "bede nogen gøre noget"
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Han bad dem væve med det samme.
grammarPoints.explanation: After bede (ask/request) + a person, the following verb is a bare infinitive with no at — unlike most other verb + infinitive constructions in Danish.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Folket klappede, fordi ingen turde sige sandheden.
grammarPoints.explanation: Fordi introduces a subordinate clause, which cannot stand alone as a full sentence. Compare with the coordinating for from story 009, which can.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Han har jo ikke noget tøj på!
grammarPoints.explanation: Jo has no direct English translation. It marks the statement as something the speaker believes is — or should be — obvious or already known to the listener.
The blive + adjective pattern for change of state appears again here, reinforcing the construction first introduced with blev gift in story 008.
